content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 18,
1994
Randy Riddle
Deputy City Attorney
Room 206 City Hall
San Francisco,
CA
94102—4682
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-94-220
Dear Mr. Riddle:
You have requested advice concernin9 the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).’!
Because your question
is general in nature,
we will treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
!
QUESTION
You have asked the proper method of reporting payments made
to defray the costs of printing ballot arguments supporting or
opposing ballot measures when the individual or entity which pays
for printing of the argument is different than the person who
signs or co—signs the argument.
You have asked how payments of
$99 or less,
payments between $100 and $999,
and payments of
$1,000 or more should be reported in the following circumstances:
1.
A committee
(as defined in the Act)
pays for a ballot
argument signed/co-signed by an individual who is not a candidate/
officeholder;
2.
A committee pays for a ballot argument signed/co-signed
by a candidate/officeholder;
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (C) (3).)
428 J Street. Suite S0()
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95S04-0S07
•
(916) 322-566()


Randy Riddle
Our File No.
1—94—220
Page
2
3.
A committee pays for a ballot argument signed/co-signed
by another committee; and
4.
An individual pays for a ballot argument signed/co—
signed by a committee.
You have also asked if the answer depends in any way on
whether the committees are controlled by a candidate/officeholder
or are primarily formed to support the ballot measure.
CONCLUS ION
Payments made to defray the costs of printing ballot
arguments supporting or opposing a ballot measure are expenditures
under the Act.
Therefore,
such payments must be counted for
purposes of determining whether an individual or entity has
qualified as a committee.
Once qualified,
a committee must
disclose all expenditures.
As discussed below,
payments made by any committee which is
not primarily formed to support or oppose the measure will also be
deemed “contributions” or “independent expenditures” under the Act
depending on the nature of the payments.
Payments which are
contributions must also be reported by the committee receiving
them.
Expenditures of $100 or more must be itemized on a
committee’s campaign statements.
Committees must itemize
contributions if $100 or more is received in a calendar year from
a single source.
Expenditures and contributions under $100 are
reported in a lump sum.
Payments which are contributions or
independent expenditures may also trigger special reports, e.g.,
late contribution reports,
late independent expenditure reports,
and supplemental independent expenditure reports.
ANALYSIS
The Act and Commission regulations define the term
“expenditure” to mean any monetary or non—monetary payment which
is made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election
of a candidate or candidates,
or the qualification or passage of
any measure.
(Section 82025; Regulation 18225.)
In addition,
Regulation 18225 provides that the term “expenditure” includes any
payment made by:
(A)
A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was made
for personal purposes unrelated to his or her
candidacy or status as an officeholder;
(B)
A controlled committee;
(C)
An official committee of a political
party,
including a state central committee,
county


Randy Riddle
Our File No.
1-94—220
Page
3
central committee,
assembly district committee or
any subcommittee of such committee; or
(D)
An organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes....
Regulation 18225(b).
Payments made to defray the costs of publishing ballot
arguments are deemed to be for the purpose of influencing the
voters for or against passage of the measure and are
“expenditures” under the Act.
(Winkler Advice Letter,
No. A-87-
023.)
Thus,
such payments must be counted for purposes of
determining if an individual or entity qualifies as a “committee”
under the Act.
The term “committee” is defined in Section 82013
to mean:
any person or combination of persons who
directly or indirectly does any of the following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar
year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar
year;
or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars
($10,000)
in a calendar year to or at the
behest of candidates or committees.
Expenditures which are made “at the behest” of a candidate!
officeholder or committee are contributions to the candidate!
off iceholder or committee.
(Section 82015; Regulation 18215.)
An
expenditure is made “at the behest” of a candidate or committee if
it is made under the control or at the direction of,
in
cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with,
or at
the request or suggestion of a candidate, controlled committee,
3
!
official committee of a political party,
or organization formed or
existing primarily for political purposes.
(Regulation 18215(b).)
Therefore,
an expenditure which is made to or at the behest
of a committee primarily formed to support or oppose a ballot
measure,
or at the behest of a candidate controlling such a
committee, will also be deemed a contribution to the committee.
However, it should be noted that an expenditure made to publicize
a candidate’s endorsement of or opposition to a ballot measure is
not a contribution to the candidate unless the endorsement message
3/
A controlled committee is one which is controlled directly or
indirectly by a candidate or which acts jointly with a candidate,
or controlled committee in connection with the making of
expenditures.
A candidate controls a committee if he, his agent
or any other committee he controls has a significant influence on
the actions or decisions of the committee.
(Section 82016.)


Randy Riddle
Our File No.
1—94-220
Page
4
expressly advocates the election or nomination of the candidate.
(Wax v. Fair Political Practices Commission, United States
District Court,
Eastern District of California,
Case No.
CIV.
S—
90—1232—LKK.)
Expenditures made to defray the cost of publishing ballot
arguments which are not made to or at the behest of a committee
primarily formed to support or oppose the ballot measure are
“independent expenditures,” as defined in Section 82031:
“Independent expenditure” means an expenditure
made by any person in connection with a
communication which expressly advocates the
election or defeat of a clearly identified
candidate or the qualification, passage or defeat
of a clearly identified measure,
or taken as a
whole and in context,
unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election but which is not
made to or at the behest of the affected candidate
or committee.
(Emphasis added.)
Section 84211 requires that expenditures of $100 or more be
itemized on a committee’s campaign statements.
Contributions
totaling $100 or more received from a single source during a
calendar year must also be itemized.
Contributions and
expenditures under $100 are disclosed in a lump sum.
Contributions and independent expenditures may also trigger
certain special reporting requirements.
F’or specific information
concerning committee reporting requirements,
including any special
reports required, please refer to the Commission’s information
manuals on campaign disclosure.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
(z,tI-
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission August 18, 1994 Randy Riddle Deputy City Attorney Room 206 City Hall San Francisco, CA 94102—4682 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-94-220 Dear Mr. Riddle: You have requested advice concernin9 the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’! Because your question is general in nature, we will treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). 2 ! QUESTION You have asked the proper method of reporting payments made to defray the costs of printing ballot arguments supporting or opposing ballot measures when the individual or entity which pays for printing of the argument is different than the person who signs or co—signs the argument. You have asked how payments of $99 or less, payments between $100 and $999, and payments of $1,000 or more should be reported in the following circumstances: 1. A committee (as defined in the Act) pays for a ballot argument signed/co-signed by an individual who is not a candidate/ officeholder; 2. A committee pays for a ballot argument signed/co-signed by a candidate/officeholder; 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (C) (3).) 428 J Street. Suite S0() • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95S04-0S07 • (916) 322-566() Randy Riddle Our File No. 1—94—220 Page 2 3. A committee pays for a ballot argument signed/co-signed by another committee; and 4. An individual pays for a ballot argument signed/co— signed by a committee. You have also asked if the answer depends in any way on whether the committees are controlled by a candidate/officeholder or are primarily formed to support the ballot measure. CONCLUS ION Payments made to defray the costs of printing ballot arguments supporting or opposing a ballot measure are expenditures under the Act. Therefore, such payments must be counted for purposes of determining whether an individual or entity has qualified as a committee. Once qualified, a committee must disclose all expenditures. As discussed below, payments made by any committee which is not primarily formed to support or oppose the measure will also be deemed “contributions” or “independent expenditures” under the Act depending on the nature of the payments. Payments which are contributions must also be reported by the committee receiving them. Expenditures of $100 or more must be itemized on a committee’s campaign statements. Committees must itemize contributions if $100 or more is received in a calendar year from a single source. Expenditures and contributions under $100 are reported in a lump sum. Payments which are contributions or independent expenditures may also trigger special reports, e.g., late contribution reports, late independent expenditure reports, and supplemental independent expenditure reports.
